whatItCovers.text: TSOA offers tiered benefits including a one-time $250 amount, up to $500 annually for Step 2, and an average of about $928 per month (not to exceed $5,568 in six months) for Step 3, covering services like caregiver respite, personal assistance, home-delivered meals, and specialized medical equipment.

As of the most recent information, enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than processed for immediate services.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, and home health aide help (bathing, walking, feeding) — but aide help is only covered while skilled care is also being provided.
To qualify, a person must be homebound (leaving home is hard or not recommended) and need part-time or intermittent skilled care, certified after a face-to-face assessment by a provider.
Covered home health services cost $0; durable medical equipment has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a doctor or provider has certified you as homebound.

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: You are 79 (meeting the age requirement), live in Snohomish County (one of the four counties with PACE coverage), and your needs with mobility, eating, and bathing suggest a level of care PACE is designed for. These all align with PACE's basic criteria.

whatItCovers.text: PACE coordinates and covers all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care, including personal care, home care, primary and specialty medical care, prescription drugs, nursing home care if ever needed, dental, transportation, and more, through an interdisciplinary team.

Eligibility generally requires being 55 or older, living in a PACE service area, needing nursing-home level of care as certified by the state, and being able to live safely in the community with PACE's help.
Snohomish County has 21 ZIP codes covered by Providence PACE; whether your specific ZIP code is included must be checked directly.
If you have Medicaid, there is no monthly premium; if you have Medicare only, there is a monthly premium (amount quoted by the PACE organization).

nextSteps: Check whether your specific ZIP code in Snohomish County is within the Providence PACE service area by calling (206) 320-5325.
Ask about the state's functional (nursing-facility level of care) assessment and how it's scheduled.
Gather financial documents (income and asset statements) in case a financial eligibility review is needed.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You need help with mobility, eating, and bathing, which suggests substantial ADL needs that this program's rules consider a pass. However, it's unknown whether you are currently enrolled in or eligible for community Medicaid (CN/ABP), which is a required piece for this program.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs like bathing, dressing, eating, and mobility, plus IADLs such as meal prep and housework when ADL help is also needed.
It can also include relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and caregiver management training.

To qualify, someone generally must be found to need nursing-facility level of care and be eligible for regular community Medicaid (not institutional rules), plus have an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in, or could enroll in, community Medicaid (CN/ABP).
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) to ask about a financial application and a CARE functional assessment.
Ask whether your income and assets would fit under community Medicaid rules, since this differs from institutional Medicaid rules.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare Part A, which is one requirement. whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, and therapy services in a skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying hospital stay.

It requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (not observation status), admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.
Costs include $0 for days 1–20, $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100, and full cost after day 100 (2026 figures).

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a recent 3-day or longer inpatient hospital stay and were told you need daily skilled care afterward.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have ongoing care needs with mobility, eating, and bathing, which fits the general purpose of respite programs. However, it's unknown whether you have an unpaid family caregiver providing regular care, which is a key eligibility factor for these programs.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite offers vouchers up to $1,000 per household per year for a caregiver providing 40+ hours per week of unpaid care, used for in-home respite, adult day services, or similar options.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers short-term planned respite care, support groups, counseling, and caregiver training; in Snohomish County, respite funding through the local Area Agency on Aging (Homage Senior Services) ranges roughly $1,000–$4,362/year on a sliding fee scale.

These programs are for the benefit of an unpaid caregiver, not paid directly to the care receiver, and generally require that the caregiver and care receiver both live in Washington and cannot otherwise afford respite.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid caregiver (family member or friend) providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week.
Can you provide: unpaidCaregiver?
nextSteps: If a family member or friend is providing regular unpaid care, contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the Family Caregiver Support Program in Snohomish County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is covered by Medicare and has care needs involving mobility, eating, and toileting due to Parkinson's disease, and lives at home rather than in a nursing facility. These facts align with several of the program's basic requirements, though homebound status and a specific skilled-care need still need to be confirmed by a provider.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B can cover part-time skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide care (for things like bathing, walking, and feeding) — but aide care is only covered while also receiving skilled nursing or therapy services.

To qualify, the person must be homebound (has trouble leaving home without help or it's not recommended due to their condition, and normally can't leave without considerable effort), and a doctor must certify the need for skilled care after a face-to-face assessment.
Covered home health services cost $0; durable medical equipment has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.
This benefit does not cover 24-hour care, or custodial/personal care when that is the only care needed — it must be paired with a skilled care need.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a doctor has certified this person as homebound.
Whether there is a specific skilled nursing or therapy need that a provider has identified.
nextSteps: Ask the person's doctor about scheduling a face-to-face assessment to evaluate homebound status and skilled care needs.
If a need is found, ask the doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Cowlitz County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person needs help with toileting, eating, and mobility, which suggests a substantial level of ADL need — the kind of need this program is designed to address. However, it's not yet known whether this person is enrolled in or eligible for community Medicaid (CN/ABP), which is a required piece.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice can cover personal care help with ADLs like toileting, eating, and mobility (transfer, bed mobility, locomotion, walking), plus health-related tasks, relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training.
To qualify functionally, an assessment must find a nursing-facility level of care, and to continue the service the person must have unmet or partially met needs in at least three ADLs.
Financially, this program generally requires eligibility for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community, not institutional financial rules.

informationStillNeeded: Whether this person is enrolled in or able to enroll in community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage).
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) through regional central intake to ask about the financial application and CARE assessment process.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person has Medicare Part A, which is one requirement for this benefit. However, it's not known whether there was a recent 3-day inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing need for daily skilled care, which is a core requirement of this program.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days.

Covered costs include a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, therapy services, medical social services, medications and supplies used in the facility; days 1-20 cost $0, days 21-100 have a daily coinsurance, and days beyond 100 are the patient's full responsibility.
This is not a long-term custodial care benefit — it is specifically for short-term skilled care needs following a hospital stay.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs offer short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers through three main channels in Washington: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers, the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), and DDA waiver respite (only for developmental disabilities that began before age 18).
Lifespan Respite vouchers require an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours per week of care, with awards up to $1,000 per household per year, subject to funding availability.
FCSP offers planned short-term care (in-home, adult day center, or residential facility) plus caregiver support services, with amounts varying by local Area Agency on Aging.

Consider applying online for Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers if a caregiver provides 40+ hours per week of care.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, medical social services, and home health aide care (help with walking, bathing, and feeding) when also receiving skilled care, at no cost for covered services.

Eligibility requires a need for part-time or intermittent skilled services, homebound status as defined by Medicare, a face-to-face provider assessment, and care ordered by a provider and delivered by a Medicare-certified agency.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a doctor has certified the person as homebound
Whether there is a documented need for skilled nursing or therapy care
nextSteps: Ask the person's doctor about a face-to-face assessment and homebound certification
Ask the doctor for a referral list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Grays Harbor County

programs.whyThisMayApply: The profile shows the person is 86 (meets the 65+ age category), has needs with transferring and eating that may indicate a nursing-facility level of care, lives in Washington (Grays Harbor County), and has countable assets under $2,000 — all of which line up with several COPES screening factors. However, whether income falls within the program's Special Income Level is unknown.

whatItCovers.text: COPES layers additional home-and-community-based services on top of personal care, including adult day care, home-delivered meals, nursing services, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment, and transportation, in a private residence, adult family home, or assisted living setting.

Eligibility involves being 65+ or meeting SSI-related disability criteria, having a CARE assessment show nursing-facility level of care (including unmet needs in ADLs like transferring and eating, or cognitive impairment such as dementia plus an ADL need), and meeting resource limits of $2,000 for a single person.

informationStillNeeded: Monthly income amount, to check it against the program's income limit
nextSteps: Contact DSHS Home and Community Services (HCS) to request a CARE functional assessment
Submit financial application HCA 18-005 via washingtonconnection.org or by mail/fax to HCS-LTSS

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person's needs with transferring and eating and dementia diagnosis suggest possible substantial ADL needs, which is a factor for this program. However, it's not yet known whether the person is enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid, or exactly how many ADLs require help.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care assistance with ADLs (including transfer and eating) and IADLs, relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training, available in a home, adult family home, or assisted living setting.

To qualify, a person generally needs a CARE assessment showing nursing-facility level of care and must be eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community, or SSI/SSI-related coverage.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is enrolled in or could enroll in community Medicaid (CN/ABP)
The total number of ADLs needing assistance
Which everyday activities do they need help with?
nextSteps: Request a CARE assessment through regional HCS central intake
Check current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment status with HCA

programs.whyThisMayApply: Age 65+, possible nursing-facility level of care due to dementia and ADL needs, Washington residency, and assets under $2,000 all align with several of this program's screening factors. Monthly income, which determines the specific eligibility pathway, is still unknown.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC covers nursing facility care for those meeting nursing-facility level of care, and serves as the financial gateway to home-and-community-based waivers like COPES, PACE, and community LTSS such as CFC.

Eligibility generally requires meeting SSI-related or MAGI-based financial criteria, income at or below the Special Income Level ($2,982/month as of 1/1/2026) or a Medically Needy pathway, and resources at or below $2,000 for a single person.

informationStillNeeded: Monthly income amount to compare against Special Income Level or Medically Needy limits
nextSteps: Submit a financial application (HCA 18-005) via washingtonconnection.org, mail, fax, or in person at an HCS office
Request a CARE functional assessment through regional HCS intake

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is covered by Medicare, which is one qualifying factor, but there's no information yet about a recent 3-day inpatient hospital stay, which is required for this benefit to apply.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, and therapy services in a skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying hospital stay.

Eligibility requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days, admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days
Can you provide: recentHospitalStay?
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare will cover a skilled nursing facility stay before admission

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is over 55, has assets that appear to fall within TSOA's resource limits, and may have a nursing-facility level of care need due to dementia and ADL needs. However, monthly income is unknown, and the program's enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a waitlist.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA offers tiered benefits including one-time and annual funds and, at the highest step, a monthly amount (recently averaging $928/month) for services like caregiver assistance, personal assistance, specialized medical equipment, and health maintenance supports.
As of the current information, enrollment is paused statewide, and new applications only place a person on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than leading to immediate services.

informationStillNeeded: Monthly income amount to compare against the TSOA income limit
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections or the local Area Agency on Aging to ask about waitlist status
Ask about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds while TSOA enrollment is paused

programs.whyThisMayApply: The care receiver appears to need ongoing supervision and help due to dementia and ADL needs, which is relevant to respite programs, but it is not yet known whether an unpaid family caregiver is regularly providing this care, which is a key factor for these programs.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs such as Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000/year) and the Family Caregiver Support Program offer short-term relief care for unpaid caregivers, including in-home respite, adult day services, counseling, and caregiver training.
Lifespan Respite vouchers require an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours per week of care, while FCSP eligibility and service levels vary by local Area Agency on Aging and a TCARE screening.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, and how many hours per week
Can you provide: unpaidCaregiver?
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging (via Community Living Connections, 1-855-567-0252) to ask about respite options
If applicable, consider applying for Lifespan Respite vouchers through wapave.org

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at five programs that might help with your care needs based on what you shared: you're 70, live in your own home in Douglas County, need help with dressing, are on Medicare, have income under $1,000/month, and countable assets between $2,000 and $85,000. Because several key details aren't known yet (like whether you're on Medicaid, how many daily activities you need help with, and whether anyone helps care for you unpaid), this report can't tell you which programs you're likely to qualify for — it can only point out what's worth asking about. Please treat this as a starting point for a conversation with the actual agencies, not a decision about your eligibility.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are enrolled in Medicare, live at home (not a facility), and have indicated a care need (help with dressing), which matches the basic pattern this benefit is designed for. However, this benefit also requires being homebound and needing skilled nursing or therapy care, which we don't yet know about you.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, therapy services, and home health aide help (such as help with bathing and grooming) when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy care, at no cost for covered services, for people who are homebound and have a provider-certified need.
This benefit does not cover 24-hour care, custodial or personal care alone, or homemaker services unrelated to the care plan.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a doctor has certified you as homebound
Whether you have a documented need for skilled nursing or therapy care
nextSteps: Ask your doctor or a home health agency to complete a face-to-face assessment
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Douglas County

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've told us you need help with dressing, live in your own home in Washington, and have income and assets that may fall within program ranges discussed in this program's materials — but we don't yet know if you're enrolled in community Medicaid or how many ADLs you need help with, both of which are central to this program.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice covers personal care assistance with ADLs including dressing, bathing, eating, and more, plus IADLs, relief care, nurse delegation, PERS, and other supports, for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care and are eligible for community Medicaid.

To continue receiving CFC at reassessment, a person must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs, and financial eligibility generally requires community Medicaid (CN/ABP) or SSI-related coverage.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in or eligible for community Medicaid (CN/ABP)

This is short-term rehabilitation coverage, not a long-term custodial care benefit.

report.overallSummary: We don't yet have enough information about this adult's age, health needs, veteran status, income, assets, or county to know which Washington care programs are worth looking into. All of the programs below are included because they showed up as possibly relevant given the limited profile provided, but almost every eligibility factor is currently unknown. programs.whyThisMayApply: The profile only tells us this is an adult in Washington state. COPES is a Medicaid waiver for people 65+ (or 18-64 with an SSI-criteria disability) who need nursing-facility level care and meet income/asset limits — none of which we know yet.

whatItCovers.text: COPES layers services like adult day care, home-delivered meals, nursing services, environmental modifications, and transportation on top of Community First Choice personal care, for people in a private residence, adult family home, or assisted living facility.

Eligibility generally requires being 65+ or 18+ with an SSI-criteria disability, needing nursing facility level of care, and meeting income limits (Special Income Level $2,982/month in 2026) and resource limits ($2,000 single).

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: We don't know this adult's age, county, or care needs, all of which determine whether PACE could be relevant.

whatItCovers.text: PACE coordinates all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care, plus other needed services, for people who need nursing-home-level care but can live safely in the community.

Eligibility generally requires being 55 or older with a disability determination (or 65+), living in a PACE service area, and needing nursing-facility level of care; in Washington, PACE service areas cover only parts of King, Pierce, Spokane, and Snohomish counties.

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at five programs that may be worth exploring for your mom, an 83-year-old widow in Clallam County with Parkinson's, mobility and toileting needs, and Medicare coverage. To be clear, this tool cannot confirm eligibility for any program — only the administering agency can make that determination. Several key details are still missing (like whether she's on Medicaid, exactly how many daily activities she needs help with, whether anyone provides her unpaid care, and her recent hospital stay history), so some of these results are preliminary. Please use the follow-up questions below to gather more information, and consider talking with a local Area Agency on Aging or DSHS office for a full assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your mom is enrolled in Medicare and has care needs related to Parkinson's, mobility, and toileting, which could indicate a need for skilled or personal care at home. This program is designed for people in her general situation, though homebound status and a specific skilled-care need still need to be confirmed by a provider.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, and home health aide care (when paired with skilled care), at no cost for covered services.
Eligibility requires a homebound status determination, a face-to-face provider assessment, and care ordered by a provider and delivered through a Medicare-certified agency.
This benefit does not cover 24-hour care, custodial-only personal care, or homemaker services unrelated to the care plan.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a doctor has certified her as homebound
Whether she has a documented need for skilled nursing or therapy care
nextSteps: Talk with her doctor about scheduling a face-to-face assessment for homebound certification.
Ask the doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Clallam County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your mom is over 55, and her income and asset levels appear to fall within TSOA's financial limits based on the ranges you provided. Her mobility and toileting needs may align with the nursing-facility level of care standard this program uses. However, TSOA currently has a statewide enrollment pause, so new applicants go on a waitlist rather than receiving immediate services.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is designed for people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic CN/ABP Medicaid, with income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid.

Benefits are structured in three steps, ranging from a one-time $250 amount to an ongoing average of $928/month (up to $5,568 over six months) for services like personal care, respite, home-delivered meals, and specialized equipment.
As of the current information, enrollment is paused statewide since December 1, 2025, and new applicants join a first-come, first-served waitlist with no announced reopening date.

nextSteps: Apply through washingtonconnection.org or the paper TSOA application to establish a place on the waitlist, since a request date matters even during the pause.
Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about non-Medicaid respite funds while waiting.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your mom is 83 and needs help with toileting and mobility, which could relate to the activities-of-daily-living needs this program looks at. whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care assistance with ADLs such as toilet use, transfers, and mobility, along with IADL help, health-related tasks, relief care, nurse delegation, PERS, and caregiver management training.
To qualify functionally, a CARE assessment must find a nursing-facility level of care, and continuing CFC requires an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs.
Financially, the person must be eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan community Medicaid coverage.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover up to 100 days per benefit period of skilled nursing facility care, including room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy, and related medical services, but only following a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay.
Days 1–20 have no daily cost; days 21–100 carry a daily coinsurance ($217/day in 2026); after day 100 the patient pays all costs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The profile describes needing help with toileting, dressing, and eating, and countable assets under $2,000, which lines up with two of the four COPES eligibility factors (functional need and resource limit). COPES also requires age 65+ or an SSI-criteria disability, and income within limits — both currently unknown for this family.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Medicaid waiver that adds services on top of personal care, including adult day care/day health, home-delivered meals, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, transportation, environmental modifications, and community transition help, in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living setting.

To be considered, a person generally must be 18+ with an SSI-criteria disability or 65+, need a nursing-facility level of care, and meet income and resource limits (resources at or below $2,000 for a single person).

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and mobility, plus related supports such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training, in home or community settings.

However, COPES also requires confirmation of age or disability status, income within the Special Income Level, and countable assets within Medicaid limits — all currently unknown for this family.

whatItCovers.text: COPES layers additional home and community-based services on top of Medicaid personal care (CFC), including adult day care, home-delivered meals, nursing services, environmental modifications, and transportation, for people living at home, in an adult family home, or assisted living.
To qualify, a CARE assessment must find the person needs nursing-facility level of care, which can be shown by unmet needs in multiple ADLs such as eating, toileting, and bathing.
Financially, available income must generally be at or below $2,982/month (as of 1/1/2026) and countable resources at or below $2,000 for a single person, though a couple may have different limits.

Your reported income under $1,000/month is within the program's Special Income Level. However, this program is administered by DSHS and requires an official assessment.

whatItCovers.text: COPES layers additional home-and-community-based services on top of personal care, including adult day care/health, home-delivered meals, nursing services, environmental modifications, transportation, and help with community transitions.

Eligibility requires being 65+ or 18–64 with an SSI-criteria disability, meeting nursing-facility level of care (which can include unmet ADL needs plus cognitive impairment like memory loss or disorientation), and staying within Medicaid income and resource limits.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care assistance with ADLs including bathing, dressing, transferring, bed mobility, and locomotion, along with services like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver training.

It provides increased annual pension amounts, such as up to $29,093/year for a veteran with no dependents (effective December 1, 2025).

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are a veteran
Your service era, if applicable
Your discharge type, if applicable
Clinical documentation of need for aid and attendance
Did they serve in the U.S. military?
When did they serve in the military?
Clinical Aid & Attendance examination findings, VA Form 21-2680
nextSteps: Determine and confirm veteran status and discharge details.
If a veteran, contact a Veterans Service Organization or the VA to discuss the pension and Aid & Attendance process.
Gather military service records if applicable.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This benefit requires being substantially confined to home by a permanent disability, which hasn't been established, along with unknown veteran status, service era, and discharge type.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit is an increased monthly VA pension payment for veterans substantially confined to their home due to a permanent disability.
It provides increased annual pension amounts, such as $21,313/year for a veteran with no dependents (effective December 1, 2025).

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are a veteran
Your service era, if applicable
Your discharge type, if applicable
Whether you are substantially confined to your home by a permanent disability
Did they serve in the U.S. military?
When did they serve in the military?
nextSteps: Confirm veteran status and discharge details.
Discuss housebound status with a medical provider if a veteran.
Contact a Veterans Service Organization for help with the application if eligible.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your described care needs suggest a possible skilled or personal care need, but whether you're enrolled in Medicare and whether you live at home (versus a facility) are both unknown, and both are required for this benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, therapy services, and home health aide care (when paired with skilled care), plus durable medical equipment, at no cost for the covered services themselves.
Eligibility requires needing part-time or intermittent skilled care, meeting homebound status criteria, and having a provider certify the need after a face-to-face assessment.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you're enrolled in Medicare
Your living situation
Whether a provider has certified a skilled care need and homebound status
Where do they currently live?
nextSteps: Confirm Medicare enrollment status.
Ask a doctor about a face-to-face assessment for home health eligibility.
Clarify current living arrangement.

programs.whyThisMayApply: There's no information yet about Medicare Part A enrollment or a recent qualifying hospital stay, both of which are required for this short-term nursing facility benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers up to 100 days per benefit period of skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a certified facility after a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay.
Covered services include a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy services, and medical supplies, with cost-sharing starting on day 21.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you're enrolled in Medicare Part A
Whether you've had a recent 3-day (or longer) inpatient hospital stay with a daily skilled-care need
Can you provide: recentHospitalStay?
nextSteps: Confirm Medicare Part A enrollment.
Ask a hospital discharge planner whether a recent stay would qualify for SNF coverage, if relevant.

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: Your described care needs align with the nursing-facility level of care criterion, but your age and county are unknown, and PACE is only available in a few specific counties.

whatItCovers.text: PACE coordinates all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care plus additional services determined necessary by an interdisciplinary team, including home care, personal care, therapy, and transportation.
Eligibility requires being age 55 or older, living in a PACE service area, and needing a nursing-home level of care while being able to live safely in the community.

informationStillNeeded: Your age
Your county of residence
How old is the person who needs care?
Which Washington county do they live in?
nextSteps: Check whether your county (King, Pierce, Spokane, or Snohomish) is served by a PACE organization.
Contact a local PACE organization's intake line to discuss options.
Confirm age eligibility.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your care needs may fit the nursing-facility level of care requirement, but age, income bracket, and current Medicaid enrollment status are all unknown, and the program is also currently paused statewide with a waitlist.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA offers benefit steps up to an average of $928/month for eligible individuals or caregiver dyads, covering services like personal care, respite, and home-delivered meals.
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist.

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections or an Area Agency on Aging to ask about the current waitlist.
Ask about non-Medicaid respite options while TSOA enrollment is paused.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program supports unpaid caregivers of people already on Medicaid, but it's unknown whether you're on Medicaid, whether you have an unpaid caregiver, your age, or your living situation — all required pieces, and the program is currently paused.

whatItCovers.text: MAC offers benefit steps (up to an average of $928/month) supporting unpaid caregivers, covering respite care, housekeeping, adult day care, and home-delivered meals.

Eligibility requires the care receiver to be 55+, already enrolled in CN/ABP Apple Health, living at home, meeting nursing facility level of care, and having an unpaid caregiver age 18+; enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your ongoing care needs fit the general purpose of respite programs, but it's unknown whether you have an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, which is required for these programs.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs offer short-term relief for unpaid caregivers, such as Lifespan Respite vouchers up to $1,000/year and Family Caregiver Support Program services including in-home respite, adult day care, and caregiver training.

Lifespan Respite requires an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours/week of care; the Family Caregiver Support Program serves unpaid caregivers of adults 18+ needing care, with services tailored through a TCARE screening.

report.disclaimer: This screening is educational only. It identifies programs that appear worth investigating based on the information provided — it does not determine eligibility, provide legal or financial advice, or estimate benefit amounts. Program rules and dollar limits change; verify current details with the program's official contacts before making decisions, and consider consulting an elder-law attorney, benefits counselor, or the Community Living Connections line (1-855-567-0252).

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at eight Washington programs that may help pay for or provide care for a 91-year-old with heart disease who needs help with dressing, mobility, and eating, has income under $1,000/month, and countable assets between $2,000 and $85,000. Several programs (COPES, Apple Health LTC, CFC, PACE, TSOA) look worth a closer look based on age, functional need, and income, but key facts are still missing — especially exact countable assets, marital status, current Medicaid or Medicare enrollment, county of residence, and whether there is an unpaid family caregiver. Two programs (TSOA and MAC) currently have paused enrollment statewide with a waitlist. Because so much information is still unknown, this report should be treated as a starting point for questions to ask a caseworker, not a determination of what this family will receive.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family profile describes a 91-year-old (meeting the 65+ age rule) who needs help with dressing, mobility, and eating, which lines up with the functional (ADL) needs COPES is designed for, and the reported income under $1,000/month is within COPES' income limits. [wa-medicaid-copes#01-overview-eligibility-part-2]

whatItCovers.text: COPES can help pay for services like adult day care, home-delivered meals, nursing services, environmental modifications, transportation, and specialized medical equipment for people living in their own home, an adult family home, or assisted living.

To qualify, an assessment must find the person needs nursing-facility level of care, based on things like needing hands-on help with several daily activities such as eating, bathing, or moving around.
Resources generally must be at or below $2,000 for a single person, with some protections if there's a spouse.

informationStillNeeded: The exact countable asset amount, to compare against the $2,000 single limit
Marital status
Whether the person is already enrolled in Medicaid
Roughly what are their countable assets?
nextSteps: Contact the regional Home and Community Services (HCS) intake line to request a CARE assessment
Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) through washingtonconnection.org or by mail/fax

programs.whyThisMayApply: The described needs with dressing, mobility, and eating suggest a substantial need for help with activities of daily living, which is central to CFC's personal care services. [wa-medicaid-cfc#00-overview-eligibility]

whatItCovers.text: CFC can cover personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and moving around, plus things like meal preparation and housework when ADL help is also needed.
To qualify, the person generally needs to already be eligible for or enrolled in regular community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage).

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is currently enrolled in or eligible for Apple Health Medicaid (CN/ABP)
nextSteps: Ask HCS or an Area Agency on Aging about combining a Medicaid financial application with a CARE functional assessment

programs.whyThisMayApply: As a 91-year-old with reported needs helping with several daily activities and monthly income under $1,000, the family profile touches several of Apple Health LTC's eligibility factors (age, functional need, income), though asset details are still unknown. [wa-apple-health-ltc#00-overview-eligibility-part-1] [wa-apple-health-ltc#01-overview-eligibility-part-2]

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC can cover nursing facility care and serves as the financial gateway to home-based waiver programs like COPES, PACE, and community LTSS services.
Income eligibility generally allows available income up to the Special Income Level ($2,982/month as of 1/1/2026), with a Medically Needy pathway for people with income above that but limited assets.
Resource limits are generally $2,000 for a single person, with higher protections for a spouse still living in the community.

informationStillNeeded: The exact countable asset amount
Marital status
Current Medicaid enrollment status
Roughly what are their countable assets?
Are they enrolled in Medicaid (Apple Health)?
nextSteps: Submit application HCA 18-005 via washingtonconnection.org or by mail/fax
Call regional HCS intake to schedule the required financial interview and CARE assessment

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person lives at home and has care needs involving mobility and daily activities, which could relate to Medicare's home health benefit, but this depends on Medicare enrollment and a doctor's certification of homebound status and skilled-care need, both currently unknown. [medicare-home-health#00-overview-eligibility]

whatItCovers.text: Medicare home health can cover part-time skilled nursing care, therapy services, and limited home health aide help, but only when paired with a skilled nursing or therapy need and a homebound certification from a provider.
This benefit does not cover 24-hour care, meal delivery, or personal/custodial care alone if that is the only care needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is enrolled in Medicare
Whether a doctor has certified homebound status and a skilled care need
nextSteps: Ask the person's primary care provider whether a face-to-face assessment for home health certification would be appropriate

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program is included as a candidate, but without information about Medicare enrollment or a recent 3-day hospital stay, it's unclear whether it currently applies to this family's situation. [medicare-snf#00-overview-eligibility]

whatItCovers.text: Medicare SNF coverage can pay for short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care after a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay, for up to 100 days per benefit period.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is enrolled in Medicare Part A
Whether there has been a recent 3-day or longer inpatient hospital stay followed by a doctor's order for daily skilled care
Can you provide: recentHospitalStay?
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare would cover a SNF stay

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: As someone over 65 needing help with several daily activities, the person may meet PACE's age and functional criteria, but whether they live in one of the few Washington counties PACE serves (King, Pierce, Spokane, or Snohomish) is not yet known. [wa-pace#00-overview-eligibility]

whatItCovers.text: PACE can provide all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care plus additional services like adult day primary care, home care, personal care, therapy, and transportation, coordinated by a care team.
PACE is only available to people living in specific service areas — currently parts of King, Pierce, Spokane, and Snohomish counties.

informationStillNeeded: County and ZIP code of residence, to check if it falls within a PACE service area
Which Washington county do they live in?
nextSteps: Check the DSHS PACE coverage ZIP code list or call a PACE organization's intake line to see if the address is in a service area

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person's age, income under $1,000/month, and reported asset range fall within TSOA's higher financial limits, and the described care needs suggest possible nursing-facility level of care, but enrollment is currently paused statewide with a waitlist. [wa-tsoa#01-eligibility] [wa-tsoa#00-overview]

whatItCovers.text: TSOA can provide caregiver support and personal assistance benefits in steps, including a one-time $250 benefit, up to $500/year in step 2, and an average of $928/month in step 3 for people who qualify.
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist; there is no announced reopening date.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is currently on CN/ABP Medicaid, which would make them ineligible for TSOA
Are they enrolled in Medicaid (Apple Health)?
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the current waitlist and to be placed on it if appropriate

programs.whyThisMayApply: MAC requires the care receiver to already be enrolled in Medicaid CN/ABP and to have an unpaid family caregiver, both of which are currently unknown for this family. [wa-mac#00-overview-eligibility]

whatItCovers.text: MAC can provide caregiver support benefits in steps, similar to TSOA, including respite care, housekeeping help, and adult day care, for caregivers of people already on Medicaid.
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with a first-come, first-served waitlist, the same as TSOA.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is currently enrolled in CN/ABP Apple Health Medicaid
Whether there is an unpaid family caregiver age 18 or older
Can you provide: unpaidCaregiver?
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging to ask about MAC's waitlist status and eligibility

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person's described care needs suggest ongoing supervision may be needed, but it is not yet known whether an unpaid family caregiver is involved, which is central to these respite programs. [wa-respite-care#00-overview-eligibility]

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs like Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000/year) and the Family Caregiver Support Program can provide short-term relief care, support groups, and caregiver training for unpaid caregivers.
Lifespan Respite generally requires the unpaid caregiver to provide 40+ hours per week of care.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, and how many hours per week
Can you provide: unpaidCaregiver?
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging via Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to discuss respite options

followUpQuestions: What is the exact amount of countable assets (bank accounts, investments, etc.), and is it above or below $2,000?
Is the person currently enrolled in Medicaid (Apple Health), and if so, what type of coverage (CN/ABP, Medically Needy, or none)?
Is the person enrolled in Medicare Part A and/or Part B?
What county does the person live in?
Is the person married, and if so, what are the spouse's income and assets?
Does the person have an unpaid family caregiver (a relative or friend, age 18+) helping regularly, and if so, how many hours per week?
